ground_truth: Topic: Please select one of the following prompts for your paper 1. Our unit on language variation looks at how different dialects of English can differ in parts of their mental grammar (phonology, morphology, syntax, etc.) in systematic ways. Write a paper about systematic differences between Standard American English (SAE) and another dialect of English. The paper should include (i) what SAE and the other dialect are (e.g., who speaks it, where it is spoken, etc.), (ii) an explanation of the phenomenon you are investigating (e.g., the auxiliary system, inflectional morphology, etc.), and (iii) similarities and differences between the two dialects with respect to that phenomenon. 2. Our unit on language change looks at relatively recent innovations in the way existing forms are used in various dialects of English. Write a paper about another recent innovation in the way a particular English word is used. This paper should include (i) what the word means, (ii) the word’s grammatical properties (i.e., its syntactic distribution), (iii) who uses it (i.e., what demographic group(s)), (iv) where you think it comes from, and (v) what social connotations it has (i.e., what do you think it signals?). 3. Something else related to language variation and/or change (if you choose this option, you must discuss the topic with me before the rough draft is due). Requirements: 4-5 pages in length. The length does not include references, but please include references in your draft. Double-spaced, 1-inch margins, 12pt-font: Calibri or Times New Roman. At least 3 references to academic sources, including at least one that was not assigned for class. APA style references/citations. All work must be your own. Timeline and submission: Topic: We will discuss in class on Monday, 2/9. Rough draft (at least 2.5 pages): due at the beginning of class on Wednesday, 2/18. Remember this draft will not be graded, but your grade on the final draft will take into account whether and how you incorporated the feedback on it. Peer review: due at the beginning of class on Wednesday, 2/25 (HuskyCT). Final draft: due with cover letter at 11:59pm on Wednesday, 3/.4 (HuskyCT or hard copy). Additional info: I cannot give extensions for the rough draft or peer review. If you need an extension for the final draft, you must speak with me ahead of time. I highly encourage you to come to office hours to discuss your paper topic, go over ideas, drafts, etc. Extra credit (1pt): Alongside your paper, write and submit an annotated bibliography of your references. An annotated bibliography should include a summary and evaluation of each of your sources, and address how the source fits into your research and writing. Common questions addressed by an annotated bibliography include but are not limited to: Was this source helpful to you? How did you use this source in your project? How does it help you shape your argument? Has it changed how you think about your topic? The extra credit writing is due together with the final draft (11:59pm, 3/.4).

Topic: Please select one of the following prompts for your paper 1. Our unit on language and technology looks at the relationship between language, speech, and other channels of communication, especially those that have been made available due to relatively recent technological innovations. Write a paper discussing a specific aspect of language or communication that is relevant to this relationship. For example, you might write about the specific features that characterize communication within a particular channel, especially in comparison with spoken language, or a specific innovation that arose out of a new medium that has had an impact on the way people communicate in spoken language or in other channels. 2. In our unit about language ideology, as well as for the rest of our units afterwards, we have seen how the language use of marginalized groups is often disciplined and discriminated against due to widespread language ideologies. Write a paper discussing how the language use of a particular group in terms of nationality, race, gender, etc. is disciplined or discriminated against, and how this is related to a specific language ideology. Be sure to discuss the specific aspects of the speakers' language use that are the subject of comment or criticism. 3. Something else related to language and technology and or language ideology if you choose this option, you must discuss the topic with me before the first draft is due. Requirements: 4-5 pages in length. The length does not include the references, but please include references in your draft. Double spaced, 1-inch margins, 12pt font: Calibri or Times New Roman. At least 3 references to academic sources, including at least one that was not assigned for class. APA style references and citations. All work must be your own. Timeline and submission: Topic: we will discuss in class on Monday, 3/23. Rough draft at least 2.5 pages due at the beginning of class on Wednesday, 4/1. Remember this draft will not be graded, but your grade on the final draft will take into account whether or how you incorporated the feedback on it. Peer review: due at the beginning of class on Wednesday, 4/8 HuskyCT. Final draft: due with cover letter at 11:59pm on Wednesday, 4/15 HuskyCT or hard copy. Additional info: I cannot give extensions for the rough draft or peer review. If you need an extension for the final draft, you must speak with me ahead of time. I highly encourage you to come to office hours to discuss your paper topic, go over ideas, drafts, etc. Extra credit 1pt due with final draft: Note: This assignment will be graded on your reflection of the general writing process and human AI difference in writing. Instructions: Choose a focus question or idea from your Paper #2, for example but not limited to a claim you are developing in a body paragraph, a paragraph that feels hard to write, or a research question that you try to narrow down. Ask an AI tool such as ChatGPT, Gemini, or another AI generator to write a paragraph responding to your question or topic. Write a short reflection 250-300 words responding to these questions: What does the AI-generated paragraph get right or wrong about your question or topic? What do you notice about tone, structure, elaboration, and use of sources? How would your own approach differ in focus, depth, or style? Submission: On HuskyCT Assignment Short Paper #2 Extra credit #2 submit a single document or write in the comment section of submission. Include the prompt you use, the AI-generated paragraph, and your reflection.

This document shows the steps to follow when you are working on the peer review and attending the peer review workshop. The purpose of peer review is for you and your partner to get constructive feedback from each other that help with revising the paper, and to practice reflecting on different aspects of a writing piece along the way. Therefore, your feedback should be geared toward constructive, specific suggestions for the overall development of the paper. Important notes: Peer reviews are graded; an incomplete, superficial review reduces the point it gets. It is necessary that you attend the peer review workshop and communicate with your partners. If you have to miss a peer review workshop, please come talk to me to make it easier for the logistics, and to figure out a way for you to make up for it. Before the workshop: You will receive two drafts of papers written by your classmates, and will be indicated as a harsh reviewer for one draft. Read your partners' work. Fill out and submit Peer Review #2-1 and #2-2 on HuskyCT before the beginning of class on Wednesday, 4/8. Use Peer Review #2-2 for your harsh review. As the harsh reviewer, you are required to identify at least one issue with relevant criteria when answering Question 3–6. Be prepared to talk to your partners about your paper and your review. During the workshop: Each writer takes a turn. Divide the time equally. The writer of the piece informs their partners specific concerns that they want feedback on. The partners go through their reviews, make sure to refer to parts of the paper relevant to the review, and respond to the author's points of concern. The writer should also respond to the review whenever they find it necessary. Make copies of your reviews and send them to your writers. After the workshop: Make revision plan and revise. Final draft due 4/15, 11:59PM. Appendix: Peer review prompts: 1. The papers you are assigned to review have a number as its file name. Please type in the number here. 2. Summary: In a sentence or two, summarize the main claim of the paper in your own words. 3. Introduction and thesis: Is the introduction clear and consistent with the rest of the paper? Does the first sentence grab the attention of the reader, and is it relevant to the topic? Is there a clearly defined thesis statement? Suggest, if possible, a way to improve the introduction or thesis statement. 4. Structure: Is the paper clearly organized? Does each paragraph or section make a point that is relevant to the overall thesis, and distinct from what has already been covered? Does the author use transitions and or signposting to help guide the reader through the argumentation? 5. Evidence: Does the author present compelling evidence in favor of their position? Are there any parts of the paper where you think the author should add more evidence, or strengthen the evidence they present? Can you think of any relevant counter arguments or counter evidence that the author should consider? 6. Clarity, style, and mechanics: Were there any awkward or confusing sentences? Is the tone or formality appropriate to the assignment? Did you find any distracting punctuation, spelling, or word usage problems? Mention here any recurring problems you detect and note the page or paragraph where the issues are located. 7. Strengths: What one or two things were most rewarding for you as a reader? Which specific parts of the paper struck you as particularly strong and worth keeping or emphasizing? 8. Areas for improvement: If you were forced to cut or change one idea or cluster of sentences or paragraphs, what would it be and why? You must pick one idea or paragraph or combination of sentences.

Purpose: To help you informally reflect on the writing process. To help me tailor my feedback to your specific needs. Submission instructions: Please submit to me with the final draft of your paper. You may upload it to HuskyCT along with your paper, or write it in the comments section of the submission on HuskyCT. This is an informal reflection, so please feel free to keep it casual and brief as long as it addresses all of the points below. Please include: Goals of the paper question and argument. How you incorporated the feedback you received from me and your peers and if there was any feedback you did not incorporate, an explanation of why not. Strengths of the paper. Parts of the paper or writing process you struggled with the most. Anything else you want to share with me. Sample template: Note: you are not required to use the following template; I am including it in case you are not sure what to write. In this paper I am trying to. Given the feedback I received on the first draft, which says, I decided to. I think that the strongest parts of my paper are. What I struggled with most was. Other things you may want to keep in mind as you read this paper are. Sincerely, your name.

Final Paper: Choose the topic of one of your two short papers to expand and develop into a final paper. You may choose a new topic, as long as you discuss it with me ahead of time. Requirements: 8-11 pages in length. The length does not include references. Double-spaced, 1-inch margins, 12pt font Calibri or Times New Roman. Your paper should be structured with labeled sections see sample papers. At least 5 references to academic sources, including at least one that was not assigned for class, and at least one that was not in the short paper. APA style references and citations. All work must be your own. Timeline and submission: Due at 11:59pm on Thursday, 12/11. Submit your paper and cover letter by uploading it to HuskyCT. If you need an extension, you must speak with me ahead of time. Note: There will be no formal peer review or rough draft, but I encourage you to talk to me if you have any questions, and or to informally send me a draft, and I will be happy to provide feedback. I also encourage you to swap papers and exchange feedback with classmates. Presentation: Presentations will take place at SHH 338, not our usual classroom. Purpose: To help you prepare for your final paper by giving you an opportunity to engage more deeply with your chosen topic and generate ideas and receive feedback from your classmates. Requirements: You will present your poster on your screen in a approximately 30 minute session. The poster should be based on your final paper topic, and include both of the following: presentation of the relevant materials such as arguments, evidence, graphics, data, quotes, discussions, references, and a discussion or interactive component such as a wrapping-up discussion question, a mid-way interactive question, or a visual attention catcher. Submission: Submit the PDF file to HuskyCT by the beginning of class on the day of your presentation. Note: Check the 11/6 slides for specific tips for formatting.
